codeSnapshot.label: rouge.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from collections import Counter
from rouge_score import rouge_scorer

# ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) — Lin, 2004
# Recall-oriented: measures what fraction of reference n-grams appear in candidate
# BLEU is precision-oriented; ROUGE is recall-oriented

def rouge_n(candidate: str, reference: str, n: int) -> dict:
    """ROUGE-N: n-gram recall, precision, and F1."""
    cand_tokens = candidate.lower().split()
    ref_tokens  = reference.lower().split()
    cand_ngrams = Counter([tuple(cand_tokens[i:i+n]) for i in range(len(cand_tokens)-n+1)])
    ref_ngrams  = Counter([tuple(ref_tokens[i:i+n])  for i in range(len(ref_tokens)-n+1)])
    # Overlap: clipped to min(candidate count, reference count)
    overlap = sum(min(cand_ngrams[ng], ref_ngrams[ng]) for ng in cand_ngrams)
    ref_total  = max(1, sum(ref_ngrams.values()))
    cand_total = max(1, sum(cand_ngrams.values()))
    recall    = overlap / ref_total   # ROUGE-N recall
    precision = overlap / cand_total  # ROUGE-N precision
    f1 = (2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)) if (precision + recall) > 0 else 0.0
    return {'recall': recall, 'precision': precision, 'f1': f1}

# Example
cand = 'The cat sat on the mat'
ref  = 'The cat is sitting on the mat'
print('ROUGE-1:', rouge_n(cand, ref, 1))
print('ROUGE-2:', rouge_n(cand, ref, 2))

# Production: use rouge_score library (Google's implementation)
scorer = rouge_scorer.RougeScorer(['rouge1', 'rouge2', 'rougeL'], use_stemmer=True)
scores = scorer.score(ref, cand)
for name, score in scores.items():
    print(f'{name}: P={score.precision:.3f} R={score.recall:.3f} F1={score.fmeasure:.3f}')

# ROUGE-L: Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
# Not necessarily contiguous — more flexible than n-gram overlap
def lcs_length(x: list, y: list) -> int:
    """Dynamic programming LCS."""
    m, n = len(x), len(y)
    dp = [[0] * (n + 1) for _ in range(m + 1)]
    for i in range(1, m + 1):
        for j in range(1, n + 1):
            dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if x[i-1] == y[j-1] else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
    return dp[m][n]

lcs = lcs_length(cand.split(), ref.split())
rougeL_recall    = lcs / len(ref.split())
rougeL_precision = lcs / len(cand.split())
print(f'ROUGE-L manual — LCS={lcs}, R={rougeL_recall:.3f}, P={rougeL_precision:.3f}')

eli3: ROUGE measures how much of a reference summary is covered by a system-generated summary. While BLEU asks 'how much of what the system says is in the reference?' (precision), ROUGE asks 'how much of the reference did the system capture?' (recall).

For summarisation, recall matters more than precision: we care that the summary covers the important points from the source, even if it says a few extra things. ROUGE-1 counts word overlaps, ROUGE-2 counts two-word phrase overlaps, ROUGE-L counts the longest common subsequence.

ROUGE is the standard metric for text summarisation (CNN/DailyMail, XSum benchmarks). A ROUGE-1 F1 of 0.40 is typical for extractive summarisation; 0.20-0.30 for abstractive summarisation (which generates new words not in the source).

whenWhy.title: Text summarisation evaluation
whenWhy.body: ROUGE (Lin, 2004) was specifically designed for summarisation evaluation. The recall orientation is appropriate: a good summary should cover all important points in the reference, even if it's longer. CNN/DailyMail and XSum benchmarks use ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L as primary metrics. Extractive summarisation (selecting sentences from source) typically achieves higher ROUGE than abstractive (generating new text), because extracted sentences verbatim match reference words.

whenWhy.title: ROUGE-L for sequence structure
whenWhy.body: ROUGE-L uses Longest Common Subsequence (LCS), which does not require contiguous matches. This is more flexible than ROUGE-2 for abstractive summaries that rephrase content using the same words in different order. A summary 'cat on mat' and reference 'the cat sits on the mat' have LCS='cat on mat' (length 3), giving ROUGE-L recall=3/6=0.5. ROUGE-L captures fluency and sentence-level structure better than ROUGE-N for generative models.

whenWhy.title: Multi-reference and bootstrap evaluation
whenWhy.body: ROUGE-N with multiple references: for each n-gram in the candidate, take the maximum overlap with any single reference. This rewards candidates that partially match different references. Jackknife resampling is standard for ROUGE significance testing: remove one reference at a time, compute ROUGE on each subset, report mean ± std. Most papers report ROUGE F1 (combining precision and recall) but recall-only ROUGE is still common in older papers — always clarify which is reported.

deepDiveIntro: ROUGE-N recall counts how many n-grams from the reference appear in the candidate, clipped at the reference frequency to prevent credit for repeated n-grams, then divides by the total number of n-grams in the reference. Precision uses the candidate length as denominator. F1 combines both. ROUGE-L uses the longest common subsequence between candidate and reference as the overlap count instead of n-gram counts, giving credit for matching words that appear in the same order even if not contiguous. Lin (2004) found ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L correlate most strongly with human judgments for news summarization. [1]

ROUGE-S counts word pairs that appear in the same order with any gap between them. Combinations such as ROUGE-2 plus ROUGE-L correlate better with human assessments than any single variant. For modern abstractive summarization, BERTScore and other learned metrics outperform ROUGE in correlation with human judgment, but ROUGE remains the standard for benchmark comparison because it is reproducible without loading large models. [1]
formula: ROUGE-N = Σ Count_match(gram_n) / Σ_reference Count(gram_n)

deepDive.heading: ROUGE-N Formula
deepDive.body: For each n-gram in the reference, the clipped match count is the minimum of how many times it appears in the candidate and how many times it appears in the reference, preventing credit for repeated n-grams. Summing these clipped counts and dividing by the total number of reference n-grams gives the recall-oriented score. BLEU uses the candidate length as denominator instead, making it precision-oriented. Using ROUGE recall and BLEU precision together gives a more complete picture of generation quality. A ROUGE-1 recall of 0.6 means sixty percent of reference words appeared somewhere in the candidate. [1]

deepDive.heading: ROUGE-L: Longest Common Subsequence
deepDive.body: The longest common subsequence of two token sequences is the longest sequence of tokens appearing in both in the same relative order, with gaps allowed. ROUGE-L uses this length as the overlap count: recall equals the LCS length divided by the reference length, precision divides by the candidate length. Compared to ROUGE-2, this is more flexible because it does not require matching bigrams to be adjacent. For example, the candidate 'cat mat on' and reference 'cat is on the mat' share no contiguous bigrams but have a common subsequence of length two, giving a recall of 0.40. [1]

deepDive.heading: ROUGE vs BERTScore for Abstractive Summarisation
deepDive.body: ROUGE relies on lexical overlap — abstractive models that paraphrase rather than extract are penalised for using synonyms or different sentence structures. BERTScore uses contextual embeddings to compute similarity — semantically equivalent paraphrases score similarly. Study (Zhang et al., 2020): BERTScore correlates better with human judgments than ROUGE on abstractive summarisation tasks (XSum, CNN/DailyMail). However, ROUGE remains the standard benchmark metric because: (1) reproducible without downloading models; (2) decades of historical results allow comparison. Best practice: report both ROUGE and BERTScore for abstractive models. [1]

takeaways: ROUGE-N = Σ Count_match(gram_n) / Σ_reference Count(gram_n) — recall-oriented n-gram overlap. [1]
ROUGE-1 (words), ROUGE-2 (bigrams), ROUGE-L (LCS) are the three standard variants. [1]
ROUGE-L: longest common subsequence — captures order without requiring contiguous matches. [1]
ROUGE complements BLEU: BLEU=precision, ROUGE=recall — use both for full picture. [1]
For abstractive summarisation, BERTScore outperforms ROUGE in human correlation. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does ROUGE-1 often appear high even for poor summaries?
edgeCases.a: ROUGE-1 measures unigram (word) overlap. Common function words ('the', 'is', 'of', 'and') appear in both candidate and reference frequently, inflating ROUGE-1. Solutions: (1) Remove stopwords before computing ROUGE-1. (2) Use ROUGE-2 instead — 'the cat' matches less spuriously than 'the' alone. (3) Use recall-only ROUGE (denominator = reference length) rather than F1 — precision punishes spurious words. The rouge_score library (Google) includes stemming to reduce exact-match sensitivity. For dialogue evaluation, even ROUGE-2 can be low for good responses that use different but valid phrasing — human evaluation or BERTScore is preferred.

edgeCases.q: How does ROUGE handle multiple reference summaries?
edgeCases.a: Standard approach (Lin, 2004): compute ROUGE between the candidate and each reference individually, then take the maximum ROUGE score across references. This rewards candidates that partially match any human reference — appropriate since human summarizers have different valid choices. Some implementations average across references instead — this is more conservative and penalises candidates that match one reference but not others. The rouge_score library supports multiple references via the max aggregation. For benchmark comparisons: always use the same number of references and the same aggregation method — different choices make scores incomparable.

sources.label: Lin (2004) — ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries, ACL Workshop 2004
